# UML — Facade (SLF4J logging example)

*Generated by Claude Opus 4.8 (claude-opus-4-8) — Created on 2026-07-20*

For further enquiry please contact Akin Kaldiroglu at akin@kaldiroglu.dev

`facade-slf4j.puml` is a PlantUML **class diagram** of the SLF4J-style logging
facade in this example. `Facade-SLF4J.png` is the rendered image.

## What the diagram shows

| GoF role | On the diagram | Real SLF4J counterpart |
|----------|----------------|------------------------|
| **Facade** | `Logger` «facade» (interface) + `FacadeLogger` (impl) | `org.slf4j.Logger` |
| **Facade entry point / binding** | `LoggerFactory` «facade» | `org.slf4j.LoggerFactory` + the binding jar (`ServiceLoader`) |
| **Subsystem** | `LoggingEngine` and its backends (`ConsoleLoggingEngine`, `RecordingLoggingEngine`, `JulLoggingEngine`), plus `MessageFormatter`, `LogEvent`, `LogLevel` | Logback / Log4j 2 / `java.util.logging` + `org.slf4j.helpers.MessageFormatter` |
| **Client** | `Main` | your application |

## How to read the relationships

- `Main --> Logger` / `Main --> LoggerFactory`: the client depends **only** on the
  facade — it never references a concrete backend.
- `Logger <|.. FacadeLogger`: the facade implementation realizes the facade interface.
- `LoggerFactory o--> LoggingEngine`: the factory holds the **current binding** —
  which backend all new loggers use.
- `FacadeLogger o--> LoggingEngine`: the facade **delegates** every call to the
  subsystem, guarding on `isEnabled(...)` before formatting.
- `LoggingEngine <|.. ...Engine`: the subsystem is pluggable; swapping the backend
  leaves the client untouched.

The key teaching point (see the note in the diagram): the subsystem classes know
nothing about the facade and remain usable on their own — a **transparent facade**.

## Re-render

```bash
plantuml facade-slf4j.puml     # produces Facade-SLF4J.png
```
